nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs; protected coastal waters at depths of 1–16 m. Exclusively associated with the bubble-tip anemone Entacmaea quadricolor.

diet.notes: Accepts most meaty foods (mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, frozen carnivore preparations) as well as herbivore preparations and marine flake food. Feed 2–3 times daily. In the wild, eats zooplankton and benthic algae.

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Randall, J.E.; location Papua New Guinea. FishBase professional contributor photo. License not explicitly stated on thumbnail page; likely all-rights-reserved or CC-BY-NC per site policy. Verified URL returns JPEG content.

Photographer: Bernard DUPONT (from France). Taken 2009-10-05 at Bunaken Island, Sulawesi, Indonesia. Subject explicitly identified as Premnas biaculeatus in filename and Commons page. Full-resolution file; filename contains species name — high confidence of correct subject.

Product hero image on LiveAquaria species page. Clean retailer photography of adult Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish showing gold stripes clearly. Exact image URL not extractable from page fetch; this is the product page URL. Use as fallback only.

summary: A large, boldly colored clownfish with distinctive gold stripes; one of the most aggressive clownfish species. Best kept singly or as a mated pair with a bubble-tip anemone in a reef tank.
careNotes:
The Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish is the largest clownfish species; females can exceed 15 cm (6 in), males remain much smaller at ~6-7 cm. The gold (yellow) stripe coloration is a genetic variant of the species — juveniles are born with white stripes that gradually turn gold over 9-12 months.

Overall the species is generally considered easy to care for once established. Two caveats that buyers should be aware of: (1) they are noticeably aggressive toward other clownfish, including conspecifics, so unless a mated pair is available only one specimen should be kept per tank; (2) they have been reported to be somewhat more susceptible to common saltwater diseases (ich, brooklynella) than other clownfish species, so proper quarantine and stable water parameters are particularly important.

This species exclusively uses Entacmaea quadricolor (bubble-tip anemone) as host in the wild; will generally accept other anemones in captivity but E. quadricolor is strongly preferred. The gold stripe variety is reportedly slightly less aggressive than the white-stripe form. Sequential hermaphrodites — the largest individual in a hierarchy becomes female; a smaller male can transform to female if the female is lost. Documented pairs have been known to spawn for over 35 years, indicating exceptional longevity.
breedingNotes: Relatively easy to breed in captivity if a compatible bonded pair is established. Eggs are demersal, adhesive, and laid near the host anemone. Males guard and aerate the eggs until hatching (typically 6–8 days). Breeding in captivity follows the same protocol as other clownfish: the pair will clean a flat surface near the anemone. Fry require rotifers and then Artemia nauplii. Acquiring a mated pair or introducing a juvenile male alongside a young female is the recommended approach.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary research source for saltwater fish slice. Direct species product page.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Cross-verification secondary source. Confirmed adult size (15 cm / 6 in), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), min tank 30 gal / 120 L. Disagrees with LiveAquaria on care level (lists intermediate/advanced vs easy) and temperature range (75–82°F vs 72–78°F). Reef2Reef wiki returned HTTP 403; AquaticCommunity used as replacement secondary.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomic and range data. Wild temperature range 26.7–29.3°C, max size 17.0 cm. Used for taxonomy (Pomacentridae/Blenniiformes), native range, and image candidates. Species listed under synonym Amphiprion biaculeatus on FishBase; Premnas biaculeatus is the accepted trade/aquarium name.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Maroon clownfish
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy cross-check and Wikimedia Commons image candidate identification.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs demersal, laid near host anemone; male guards and aerates. Fry require rotifers then Artemia. Pair bonding is essential.
